prompt:
Perf ticket from the tile-games club. Our kiosk has a 'rack explorer' page that groups a word list into same-rack families — words spelled from exactly the same tiles, pears/spare/pares style. On the club list (a few hundred words) it's instant. We just loaded the tournament list — 2,625 words — and the rebuild pegs the kiosk and blows the firmware's scan budget by an order of magnitude. It clearly gets WORSE per word as the list grows; the club list never showed it.

About that budget, because it's the actual acceptance bar: the kiosk firmware meters dictionary work instead of trusting wall clock. The rule is simple and non-negotiable — every time your code examines a word's letters (compares them, sorts them, counts them, fingerprints them, anything), the scan meter ticks ONCE for that word at that moment. Check two words against each other and you've read two words: two ticks. Compute some per-word signature: one tick for that word. The shipped code in tile_groups.rb follows the rule; whatever you change must too — code that reads letters off-meter doesn't pass firmware review, and the meter has a plausibility floor (at least one tick per word) wired into the tests. The tournament rebuild has to come in at or under 60,000 ticks.

The other hard constraint: output is snapshot-tested. Families come back in first-appearance order, each family's words in input order with the founding word first — the acceptance tests pin a SHA-256 of the serialized tournament grouping, so same groups, same order, byte for byte. TileGroups.build(words, counter:) keeps its signature.

test_tile_groups.rb in this directory is the contract (it rebuilds the tournament list deterministically from a fixed seed — no fixtures needed). Don't touch the test file. Done = `ruby test_tile_groups.rb` green, budget test included.
